comments.body: For any trivial program, just use a Scanner. I've never seen Console used before, and use an InputStream/Reader when dealing with something like files.
System.console().readLine("Enter input: ") is a bit simpler to use than scanner and it also allows you to specify an input prompt message. But it might not work properly in some IDEs

answers.body:
Console class is implemented in a platform independent way to handle the console input for different Os. All OS has a console/shell but they are quite different in implementation. So Console class gives you a Java platform independent runtime class to access things like password input, etc.

Scanner is used for parsing tokens from the contents of the stream while BufferedReader just reads the stream and does not do any special parsing.

beside these you can also use datainputstream etc.

Now BufferedReader Read text from a character-input stream, buffering characters so as to provide for the efficient reading of characters, arrays, and lines. The buffer size may be specified, or the default size may be used. The default is large enough for most purposes.

Where Scanner is a simple text scanner which can parse primitive types and strings using regular expressions. A Scanner breaks its input into tokens using a delimiter pattern, which by default matches whitespace. The resulting tokens may then be converted into values of different types using the various next methods. Scanner is used for parsing tokens from the contents of the stream while BufferedReader just reads the stream and does not do any special parsing.

also check the below link it will surely help you.......

http://www.javawebtips.com/50474/

comments.body: also read the answers at stackoverflow.com/questions/2231369/…

Be careful with scanner, when you have new Scanner(System.in) and you close scanner, then System.in will be closed, too. If you want to use new Scanner(System.in) somewhere again, then trying to read user input will throw exception. Currently I am trying out JLine, what can read password to and prints given mask.

answers.body:
BufferedReader

  • Since Java 1.1
  • Throws checked exceptions
  • Can read single chars, char arrays, and lines
  • Fast

Scanner

  • Since Java 1.5
  • Throws unchecked exceptions
  • Can read lines, numbers, whitespace-delimited tokens, regex-delimited tokens
  • Difficult to read single characters

Console

  • Since Java 1.6
  • Throws unchecked exceptions
  • Not always available (e.g. if input/output is redirected, and in Eclipse)
  • Can read lines
  • Underlying reader can read single chars and char arrays (but stops at line bounds)
  • Can read passwords (i.e. read without displaying the characters)

Recommendation: Scanner

The methods for reading numbers are very useful (though beware when using nextInt() etc. followed by nextLine()). The exceptions are unchecked, so you do not have to write boilerplate try/catch blocks.

tl;dr

In Java 25+, use: IO.readln( "Enter name: " )

IO class

As of Java 25 and later, you have a fourth choice: IO class.

This class was added as part of the Paving the on-ramp project to make the learning curve more gentle on students of Java programming. See JEP 512: Compact Source Files and Instance Main Method. The class is also handy for seasoned professionals.

If desired, you can combine the prompt with the readln.

String userName = IO.readln( "Enter your name: " )

The IO class uses System.in & System.out behind the scenes, exposing convenient static methods readln, println, & print. Shortens the wordy System.out.println( … ).

For most purposes, IO supplants all three of your mentioned classes: Console, BufferedReader, and Scanner. Use Scanner only when you need its specialized parsing features (or just do the parsing yourself, as seen below).

Example

Here is a simplistic demo.

Use String#trim & String#strip to remove leading/trailing whitespace.

IO.println( Runtime.version() + " | " + Instant.now() );

IO.println( "Entering new employee record. Type 'quit' or 'exit' to end." );

String name = "";
while ( name.isBlank() )
{
    name = IO.readln( "Enter employee name: " ).trim().strip();
    if ( name.equalsIgnoreCase( "quit" ) || name.equalsIgnoreCase( "exit" ) ) System.exit( 0 );  /* 0 = success, non-zero = error */
}

LocalDate hireDate = null;
while ( Objects.isNull( hireDate ) )
{
    try
    {
        hireDate = LocalDate.parse( IO.readln( "Enter date hired: " ).trim().strip() );
    }
    catch ( DateTimeParseException e )
    {
        if ( name.equalsIgnoreCase( "quit" ) || name.equalsIgnoreCase( "exit" ) ) System.exit( 0 );  /* 0 = success, non-zero = error */
        IO.println( "Invalid date format. Use YYYY-MM-DD." );
    }
}

record Employee( String name , LocalDate hireDate ) { }
Employee employee = new Employee( name , hireDate );
IO.println( employee );

When run:

26-ea+23-2372 | 2025-11-18T01:53:55.527757Z
Entering new employee record. Type 'quit' or 'exit' to end.
Enter employee name: Basil
Enter date hired: 2025-01-23
Employee[name=Basil, hireDate=2025-01-23]

title: BufferedReader vs Console vs Scanner
body:
I'm new to Java and I would like to know what is the best choice to read user input in the console. As far as I know there are three ways to do it:

  1. Console console = System.console();
  2. BufferedReader input = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));
  3. Scanner reader = new Scanner(System.in);

Which one should I choose? Why that one and not the other ones?
